coredata.dc:title: Blue-emitting carbon quantum dots: Ultrafast microwave synthesis, purification and strong fluorescence in organic solvents
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Jie
Wu, Chunxing
Cui, Yongmei
Li, Dongxiang
Zhang, Yaojun
Xu, Jie
Li, Chunfang
Iqbal, Shahid
Cao, Meiwen
coredata.dc:description: The impurity of unpurified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) severely limits their application. In this paper, CQDs synthesized by ultrafast microwave hydrothermal method from L-cystine were purified by both dialysis and direct phase-transfer. The as-obtained CQDs in basic aqueous reaction mixture show excitation-independent fluorescence around 505 nm with a maximum excitation at 421 nm and pH-responsive emission intensity. However, after purification by dialysis the CQDs display excitation-dependent blue fluorescence from 410 to 500 nm with respect to the excitation from 310 to 430 nm. Particularly, the CQDs purified by direct phase-transfer into various organic solvents show two strong blue fluorescence peaks respectively focusing on 422–434 nm and 400–410 nm with a maximum excitation at 370–372 nm. The quantum yield of the CQDs decreases to 45.1% after a purification by dialysis from the original 82.7%, while it decreases to 57.5% after a purification of phase-transfer. The CQDs extracted in organic solvents are demonstrated to be pure small crystalline-carbon nanoparticles with chemically surface-grafted groups, while the dialyzed CQDs will physically adsorb additional L-cystine, sodium and by-product nitrate ions. The difference of surface state and solvent environment of these CQDs shall contribute to the different fluorescence property. Such strong luminescent, pure CQDs in organic solvents should have obvious advantages in further applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Microwave synthesis
Fluorescence
Purification
Phase-transfer
Surface state

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs by microwave hydrothermal carbonization
Purification of the CQDs by dialysis
Purification of the CQDs by phase-transfer
Structure analysis of the CQDs in different suspensions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence emission spectra of as-obtained CQDs after 30–90 s carbonization (exited at 365 nm); (B-C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the diluted CQDs (exited at 365 nm) (B) and the fluorescence intensity to the corresponding concentration of raw L-cystine (C); (D-E) pH-dependent fluorescence emission spectra (exited at 365 nm) (D) and the corresponding fluorescence intensity change (E) of equal concentration of CQDs at different pH solutions; (F) UV–vis spectra of the CQDs at different concentrations in diluting process.
3-D fluorescence spectrum of the as-obtained CQDs in aqueous basic mixture solution.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the dialyzed CQDs prepared by 30 s-, 60 s- and 90 s- carbonization (excited at 365 nm), (B) 3-D fluorescence spectrum of the dialyzed CQDs prepared by 30 s-carbonization, (C) TEM image with inserted size distribution diagram and (D) high-resolution TEM image of the dialyzed CQDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs extracted in hexane and CH2Cl2, as well as the residual CQDs in aqueous mixture phase after 12 h extraction by hexane or CH2Cl2, respectively (excited at 365 nm); (B) photos of the extracted CQDs in different organic solvents (E-A: ethyl acetate); (C) TEM image with inserted size distribution diagram and (D) high-resolution TEM image of the extracted CQDs.

3-D Fluorescence spectra of the CQDs extracted in ether (A), chloroform (B), hexane (C), toluene (D), CH2Cl2 (E), and ethyl acetate (F). The maximum values of z-axis (fluorescence intensity) in the patterns are labeled by color column sales.
Quantum yield measurement of as-obtained CQDs, dialyzed CQDs using Rhodamine 6G as a standard with a known quantum yield, and extracted CQDs using Quinine sulfate a standard.
XPS survey patterns of the unpurified CQDs, the dialyzed CQDs and the extracted CQDs by hexane.
XPS deconvolution of C1s (A-C), N1s (D-F) and S2p (G-K) peaks of the unpurified CQDs (A, D, G), the dialyzed CQDs (B, E, H) and the extracted CQDs by hexane (C, F, K).

Schematical structure illustration for the CQDs in aqueous mixture solution as-obtained by microwave hydrothermal method (A), after a purifaction of dialysis in water (B), and after a purifaction of phase-transfer into organic solvents (C).
Fluorescence emission peak position of the CQDs in different organic solvents.
Element molar components of three samples by XPS analysis.

head.ce:title: Blue-emitting carbon quantum dots: Ultrafast microwave synthesis, purification and strong fluorescence in organic solvents

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The impurity of unpurified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) severely limits their application. In this paper, CQDs synthesized by ultrafast microwave hydrothermal method from L-cystine were purified by both dialysis and direct phase-transfer. The as-obtained CQDs in basic aqueous reaction mixture show excitation-independent fluorescence around 505 nm with a maximum excitation at 421 nm and pH-responsive emission intensity. However, after purification by dialysis the CQDs display excitation-dependent blue fluorescence from 410 to 500 nm with respect to the excitation from 310 to 430 nm. Particularly, the CQDs purified by direct phase-transfer into various organic solvents show two strong blue fluorescence peaks respectively focusing on 422–434 nm and 400–410 nm with a maximum excitation at 370–372 nm. The quantum yield of the CQDs decreases to 45.1% after a purification by dialysis from the original 82.7%, while it decreases to 57.5% after a purification of phase-transfer. The CQDs extracted in organic solvents are demonstrated to be pure small crystalline-carbon nanoparticles with chemically surface-grafted groups, while the dialyzed CQDs will physically adsorb additional L-cystine, sodium and by-product nitrate ions. The difference of surface state and solvent environment of these CQDs shall contribute to the different fluorescence property. Such strong luminescent, pure CQDs in organic solvents should have obvious advantages in further applications.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized by microwave hydrothermal method from L-cystine were purified by dialysis in water and direct phase-transfer into organic solvents. The as-obtained CQDs in the basic reaction mixture show green excitation-independent fluorescence around 505 nm with a maximum excitation wavelength of 421 nm. However, the CQDs after purification by dialysis display an excitation-dependent blue fluorescence, the maximum emission intensity focuses on the position of 365 nm excitation and 450 nm emission. Particularly, the CQDs purified by phase-transfer show two strong blue fluorescence peaks respectively focusing around 422–434 nm and 400–410 nm in organic solvents with a maximum excitation at 370–372 nm.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted great interest due to their outstanding advantages such as photo stability, chemical stability, biocompatibility, low toxicity and low cost as compared with other optical materials [1–9], so they are extensively studied in the fields of light-emitting materials [10–13], sensors [14–18], bio-imaging [19–21] and labeling [22], as well as promoters for solar cells [23].

Currently, multiple color CQDs have been synthesized by “top-down” and “bottom-up” strategies. In the former case, CQDs are produced from large precursors such as nanosized carbon [24], active carbon [25–27] and powder graphite [28]. The “bottom-up” strategies have more selections in carbon resources such as amino acid [29,30], proteins [31,32], peptides [29], sugars [33,34], fruit juices [35–37], plant gums [38,39] or hydrophilic polymers [40]. Among the “bottom-up” strategies pyrolysis carbonization has exhibited advantages of operation simplicity, high efficiency and so on [14,34,41–43]. For example, high-quality CQDs can be synthesized by hydrothermal methods at 300 °C from hydrophilic compounds [29], at 160–220 °C from orange pericarp extraction [44], or at 120 °C from orange juice [35]; meanwhile, hydrophobic CQDs can be obtained by pyrolysis of ascorbic acid in ethanol under reflux [45]. Besides, Xiong and his coworkers reported a solvent-controlled synthesis method for CQDs with a wide color gamut and a narrow emission peak in solvothermal reactions at 210 °C [46]. Particularly, the microwave hydrothermal carbonization can provide series of CQDs toward miscellaneous applications by low cost, simple reaction equipments [30–33,38,47], so they are considered as fast and efficiency techniques to face the challenge of large-scale producing [37,38,48–51]. Many carbon-containing small molecules and polymers can be used as presursors in the microwave methods including citric acid [48,52], glycerol [50,53], polyethylene glycols [54], amino acids [30,55–57], saccharides [33,58–60], nature polysaccharides [38,49,61–63], peptide and polyamine [64,65]. Yu and Wang prepared highly green-fluorescent CQDs from phthalic acid and triethylenediamine [66]; Liu and Wang et al. reported water-soluble luminescent carbon dots as a biocompatible fluorescent ink from citric acid and urea [67]; Zhang and his coworkers synthesized crystalline red- or green- emitting CQDs in glycerol or ethylene glycol from ammonium citrate tribasic and formamide [68].

However, the purification of the obtained CQDs is still a critical problem till now. First, filtration and centrifugation can only remove aggregates or large particles and cannot provide a really pure sample [38,39,48,49,60–63]. Second, though dialysis [30,50,57,64–66] and silica gel chromatography [68] have been used for purification, they cannot adopt to a scalable amount. So that, large-scale producing of the CQDs were usually not purified [37,51], and in this case the CQDs factually were suspended in a mixture solution or dispersed in a mixture powder of raw materials and by-products, and such impurity will severely limit their application. In this paper, CQDs were synthesized by microwave hydrothermal method from L-cystine, then dialysis and direct phase-transfer were introduced to purify the as-obtained CQDs, respectively. The CQDs before purification showed excitation-independent green fluorescence with pH-responsive emission intensity. However, after purification by dialysis in water or phase-transfer into organic solvents (also can be named as extraction) they emitted blue fluorescence: (1) the purified CQDs by dialysis showed excitation-dependent fluorescence from 410 nm to 500 nm as the excitation wavelength changed from 310 to 430 nm; (2) the purified CQDs by phase-transfer showed two blue emission peaks respectively around 422–434 nm and 400–410 nm in different organic solvents with largely enhanced emission intensity. The surface states of the different CQDs were analyzed to explain the difference of fluorescence property.

CQDs were synthesized from L-cystine by microwave hydrothermal method [30]. In detail, 1.0 g L-cystine (99.5%, Aladdin, China) was dissolved in 10 mL NaOH solution (0.95 mol/L), followed by an ultrasonication of the system to be clear. Then, the reaction system was controlled at 150 °C for 30–90 s under stirring in an intelligent microwave chemical synthesizer (XH-200A, 800 W, Xianghu Technologies, Beijing, China). The samples were obtained after a naturally cooling to room temperature.

The as-obtained suspension of CQDs was diluted with HCl solution to different pH for pH-dependent fluorescence measurement. In detail, 0.5 mL of as-obtained CQDs was mixed with different volume of 1 mol/L HCl solution and then with a certain volume of water to get a total volume of 4.5 mL. The final pH of the mixture was measured by a pH meter (PB-10, Sartorius).

The as-obtained CQDs were added with an equal volume of organic solvents including hexane, CH2Cl2, toluene, ether, chloroform and ethyl acetate (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. China), respectively. The mixture was vigorously stirred for 12 h, and then the organic phase was separated by a 10 mL separating funnel (Beijing Synthware Glass Inc.).

Fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–vis spectroscopy were collected on an F-2700 spectrometer (HITACHI High-Technologies Corporation, Japan) and a PERSEE TU1901 spectrometer (Beijing Purkinje General, China), respectively. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were obtained on a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on an ESCALab220i-XL electron spectrometer (VG Scientific) using a 300 W Al Ka radiation at about 3 × 10−9 mbar.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs by microwave hydrothermal carbonization
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, CQDs were synthesized by microwave carbonization of a carbon source L-cystine [30]. The reactant concentration was relatively high: approximately, L-cystine was 0.417 mol/L and NaOH as a catalyst was 0.95 mol/L. The color of the obtained systems changed from light yellow to dark brown as prolonging the carbonization time in the microwave synthesizer [30]. The obtained samples showed green fluorescence as irradiated by 365 nm light, so the fluorescence emission spectra of these samples after carbonization of 30 s, 60 s and 90 s were characterized as shown in Fig. 1A. It is found that the samples show a fluorescent emission around 500 nm and the sample prepared after 30 s-carbonization has the largest emission intensity. The sample photos inset in Fig. 1A shows light yellow color and green fluorescence under daylight and 365 nm UV light, respectively.

Subsequently, the sample prepared by 30 s-carbonization was further diluted by water to different concentration; the fluorescence emission spectra and intensity concerning the corresponding concentration (based on L-cystine) were shown in Fig. 1B and C. It is found that the fluorescence emission wavelength does not change at different concentrations and the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases in the diluting process. Moreover, the intensity curve slope in Fig. 1C decreases as the concentration increasing, which may be due to the concentration quenching effect of fluorescent substances. In addition, CQDs have been published to have pH responsive fluorescence and to be used as pH sensors [69], they have not only displayed pH-dependent emission intensity, but also shown pH-related maximum emission wavelength by red- or blue- shifts. Herein, Fig. 1D and E show the emission spectra and intensity change of the CQDs of same concentration under different pH, they display similar emission peaks around 500 nm without wavelength shift (Fig. 1D), but the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases as lowering solution pH (Fig. 1E). Such a pH-response of the CQDs can be explained by the losing of surface charge due to the protonation of carboxyl groups [69,70]. Furthermore, UV–vis absorption spectra of the as-obtained sample and the diluted samples were shown in Fig. 1F. It is found that the all samples do not show obvious absorption as the wavelength >350 nm, and they can be differentiated only as the wavelength <350 nm.

Although the UV–vis absorption curves of the CQDs are similar to those in the published works [29,39,65], it is difficult to find a proper excitation wavelength for fluorescence emission. So, 3-dimensional (3-D) fluorescence spectra were measured as shown in Fig. 2. It is found that the excitation-emission pattern shows a conical mountain peak with maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 421 nm and 505 nm (intensity counts: 350). When changing excitation wavelength, the emission peak stably focuses around 505 nm, no more than 5 nm shift; this is different to those reported CQDs with tunable excitation-dependent photoluminescence [30,38,39,49,55–60,64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Purification of the CQDs by dialysis
ce:section.ce:para: The as-obtained CQDs by the microwave synthesis were factually dispersed in a mixture solution of un-carbonized L-cystine, NaOH and by-products, so that the purification of such mixture samples was very important for next applications. In experiments, they were first purified by dialysis with ultrapure water similarly to those in the published works [30,57,64–66]. The obtained sample after dialysis remains a slight yellow color, but its fluorescence spectra ( Fig. 3 A) obviously differs to those before dialysis (Fig. 1 A). First, the fluorescence emission peak blue-shifts from 505 nm to around 455 nm, i.e. from green to blue emitting. Second, the maximum emission intensity of the 30 s-carbonization sample decreases by a half, while the samples of 60 s- and 90 s- carbonization remain about 75% emission intensity. 3-D fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 3B) of the dialyzed 30 s-carbonization sample shows an elliptical cone peak with the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 365 nm and 450 nm. Noticeably, when the excitation wavelength changes from 310 nm to 440 nm, the maximum emission wavelength correspondingly shifts from 410 nm to 500 nm, showing excitation-dependent fluorescence.

Moreover, TEM image in Fig. 3C shows well-dispersed nanoparticles with a size range of 2–4 nm and the size distribution diagram in Fig. 3C denotes an average diameter of 2.8 nm. The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 3D, also see Supplementary material, Fig. S1) shows a clear crystal lattice of carbons, the calculated spacing of the lattice fringe is 0.21 nm based on the labeled scale, attributing to the <100> facet of graphite [51,71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Purification of the CQDs by phase-transfer
ce:section.ce:para: In particular, a direct phase-transfer method was introduced to purify the CQDs for the first time herein. After a continuous stirring with hexane or CH2Cl2 for 12 h, the residual CQDs in aqueous solution phase only remain approximately 20–25% fluorescence emission intensity ( Fig. 4A) as compared with the original value (Fig. 1A). So it is believed that the major quantum dots are extracted into the organic phase. In addition, the fluorescence emission wavelength of the extracted CQDs in hexane (Fig. 4A) blue-shifts to 400–470 nm with two strong peaks, showing remarkably enhanced intensity by approximately 12 times; the emission intensity in CH2Cl2 increases to a value of 3 times. These results may imply that the CQDs not only can be extracted into organic solvents, but also can emit stronger blue fluorescence there. Thus, several commonly used organic solvents immiscible with water, including ethyl acetate, toluene, ether, and chloroform, were selected to purify CQDs by the phase-transfer method. As shown in Fig. 4B, the photos of the extracted CQDs in different organic solvents demonstrate that these suspensions of the CQDs under daylight are clear and transparent, while under 365 nm UV light they emit strong blue fluorescence in toluene, ether, chloroform, and hexane, but weak fluorescence in ethyl acetate and CH2Cl2. UV–vis absorption spectra of the CQDs extracted in different organic solvents (Fig. S2) indicate that as the wavelength >350 nm the absorption curves don’t show obvious difference with each other, similar to that in aqueous mixture solution; while a tiny absorbance difference appears as the wavelength <350 nm in different organic solvents, the absorbance descending order is in toluene, ether, hexane, chloroform, CH2Cl2, and ethyl acetate.

Fig. 4 C shows a TEM image of the extracted CQDs. It is obvious that the spherical nanoparticles are well-dispersed with a particle size range from less than 1 nm to near 4 nm. The inserted size distribution diagram implies an average size of 2.6 nm. The size difference compared to that of the dialyzed CQDs can be ascribed to two reasons: (1) some small CQDs were found to be capable to permeate the dialysis bag because water outside the dialysis bag was found to have fluorescence; (2) small nanoparticles should be easier to be phase-transferred into organic solvents to our knowledge. Moreover, a high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 4D (also in Fig. S3) shows a clear crystal lattice of carbons and the spacing of the lattice fringe is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the <100> facet of graphite [51,71].

From Fig. 4A, the fluorescence emission excited at 365 nm in hexane and CH2Cl2 was found of much difference in height and shape of the curves, so 3-D fluorescence spectra of the CQDs extracted in different organic solvents were measured to reveal the excitation/emission properties in detail. Some information can be collected from these 3-D fluorescence patterns in Fig. 5A-F: (1) The excitation wavelength is around 370–372 nm in organic solvents ( Table 1). (2) From the fluorescence intensity counts, the CQDs in ether show the largest value; those in chloroform and hexane also give satisfying data, followed by toluene and CH2Cl2, those in ethyl acetate display the minimum emission. (3) The fluorescence intensity in organic solvents is much larger than that in the aqueous basic mixture solution. The value of the maximum fluorescence intensity (counts: 4400) in ether is approximately 12 times to the value of the CQDs before purification (counts: 350), while even in ethyl acetate the value is near three times. (4) The CQDs in organic solvents show two main emitting peaks and the fine emission wavelengths differ from each other due to different solvent polarity [72–75]. As shown in Table 1, the first emission peak wavelength of the CQDs in polar chloroform, CH2Cl2, and ethyl acetate are relatively long (≥ 428 nm), whereas that for nonpolar ether and hexane are short (≤ 424 nm). Though toluene is a nonpolar solvent, the first emission wavelength is much longer than others; this can be ascribed to π-bond interaction with the CQDs. The gap between these two peaks in a same solvent is around 22 nm except toluene (29 nm). These results indicate that the solvent polarity affect the fluorescence property of the CQDs both in emission wavelengths and emission intensity [2,73–76]. We shall conclude that the phase-transfer of the CQDs from aqueous mixture solution to organic solvents can not only provide suspensions of pure CQDs, but also dramatically enhance the fluorescence emitting. Moreover, these CQDs in organic solvents were found to have better long-term stability than those in aqueous mixture solution.

In addition, the “relative” quantum yield of the as-obtained CQDs, dialyzed CQDs and extracted CQDs were measured by comparing it to a standard with a known quantum yield [77–80]. As shown in Fig. 6, the quantum yield of the as-obtained CQDs is calculated to be 82.7% and that of the dialyzed sample is 45.1% using Rhodamine 6G as a standard, while the quantum yield of the extracted CQDs is 57.5% using Quinine sulfate as a standard. These values of quantum yields are relatively high than those data in the published literatures and the value difference of these samples should be ascribed to solvent environment and surface state of the CQDs [72–75].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure analysis of the CQDs in different suspensions
ce:section.ce:para: According to the published work [26], the electrophoretic separation of candle soot demonstrates that the fluorescence properties are related to surface charge of the CQDs, in which the faster moving carbon nanoparticles with shorter wavelength emitting have a higher negative charge due to the presence of carboxylic acid groups. Moreover, the surface state of the CQDs can be engineered to achieve controllable luminescence by surface charging [77–79] and surface oxidation [80]. Herein, the CQDs before purification, after dialysis in aqueous suspension, or extracted into organic solvents display different fluorescence property in the emission/excitation wavelengths and the emission intensity. So, we think that such a difference should be related with the surface state of the CQDs besides the solvent environment.

In our experiments, three samples including the CQDs before and after dialysis, the extracted sample in hexane, were dried and characterized by XPS. The elemental survey patterns are shown in Fig. 7 and the element contents provided by XPS analysis are listed in Table 2. Obviously, both the unpurified CQDs and the dialyzed CQDs are composed of C, O, N, S and Na. Obviously, apart from the CQDs themselves the unpurified sample (before dialysis) should contain raw material L-cystine, NaOH and by-products generated in carbonization, so that they ought to have all above elements. Subsequently, after dialysis the majority of unreacted L-cystine, NaOH and small molecule by-products shall permeate out of the dialysis bag, so the fact that the dialyzed sample still contains all above elements may reveal a surface state of these carbon dots, i.e. N and Na may be chemically or physically attached on the CQDs’ surface. In the other case, the purified CQDs by the extraction of hexane contain only major C (~87.97%), subsidiary O (~11.90%), and tiny sulfur (0.13%); such a component result may represent a pure carbon core with surface-grafted groups, which is much different to the sample obtained by similar microwave hydrothermal methods [81].

The binding energy patterns of C, N and S are shown in Fig. 8. From the deconvolution of C1s in Fig. 8A–C, the binding energy peak at 284.9 eV corresponds C˭C bonds in the carbonized products. The peak at 286.0 eV should correspond to C-N/C-S/C-O bonds in the sample before and after dialysis (Fig. 8A and B), while it should represent C-S/C-O bonds in the extracted sample (Fig. 8C) [51,61]. The presence of C-O bonds in the extracted sample implies C-OH or C-O-C groups on the CQDs’ surface. The binding energy peak higher than 288 eV should be ascribed to carbonyl (C˭O) and carboxylic groups (-COOH/-COONa) [68,81]. The unpurified CQDs (Fig. 8A) show more carboxyl/carboxylic carbon atoms because of the presence of unreacted L-cystine, but the content of carbonyl/carboxylic groups in the extracted CQDs is very low as compared with the dialyzed sample, so it is deduced that a large amount of carbonyl/carboxylic groups are physically adsorbed on the dialyzed CQDs’. In addition, the element N and Na, possibly L-cystine sodium salt, may be physically adsorbed on the nanoparticle surface in the dialyzed sample. The physically adsorbed components cannot be extracted into organic solvent in the phase-transfer process.

From the deconvolution of N1s in Fig. 8D and E, the peaks at 399 eV, 401 eV and 407 eV should ascribed to amino (-NH2), ammonium (NH4 +) and nitrate (NO3 -) [66], the latter two species shall be generated as by-products in the microwave carbonization process; while Fig. 8F demonstrates the absence of N in the extracted sample, which verifies that the N element is physically adsorbed on the surface of the dialyzed CQDs. In Fig. 8G and H, the peaks at 163.5 eV and 164.9 eV are ascribed to the S2p1/2 and S2p3/2 in C-S groups of unreacted/adsorbed L-cystine. However, the peaks at 167.4 and 168.6 eV in Fig. 8H should be attributed to sulfinate group, while those tiny peaks at 168.9 eV and 170.1 eV in Fig. 8K should be ascribed to sulfonate group. This result also denotes that sulfur is chemically attached on the surface of carbon nanoparticle, not doped in the carbon cores. Moreover, the deconvolution of O1s (Fig. S4) show that there are three O states in the CQDs before and after dialysis, the binding energy peak at 535 eV may be ascribed to the carboxylic anion, while the peaks at ~533 and ~531 eV can possibly be attributed to C-O and C˭O bonds, respectively.

Therefore, it is concluded that the CQDs purified by the extraction of organic solvents should be a pure carbon core with surface-grafted hydroxyl, ether, carbonyl, carboxylic groups and tiny amount of sulfonate groups. Whereas, the dialyzed CQDs should have additional physically adsorbed L-cystine, as well as sodium and nitrate ions. Overall, the environment and surface structure of three CQDs can be schematically illustrated in Fig. 9. First, the CQDs as-obtained by the microwave synthesis shall attach surface-grafted groups and physically adsorbed components; they are suspended in an aqueous basic reaction mixture of L-cystine, NaOH, by-product ammonium and nitrate ions etc.; meanwhile, the ratio of different S oxidation indicates a by-product of cysteine and a thiol group chemically grafted on the CQDs’ surface. Second, the CQDs after dialysis in water attach chemically surface-grafted groups and physically adsorbed components of L-cystine, sodium and nitrate ions. In this stage, thiol group should be oxidized to sulfinate group. Third, the CQDs extracted into organic solvents only remain the chemically surface-grafted groups including hydroxyl, ether, carbonyl, carboxylic and tiny sulfonate groups. The surface state of the CQDs in different solvent environment will contribute the different excitation and emission property according to the fluorescence mechanism of carbon dots [77,80–82].

In this work, CQDs were synthesized by the ultrafast microwave hydrothermal carbonization. The as-obtained CQDs in an aqueous basic mixture solution display pH response, excitation-independent fluorescence with the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of 421/505 nm. After purification by dialysis in water, the emission intensity dramatically decrease, and the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths shift to 365/450 nm with excitation-dependent fluorescence. Whereas, after purification by phase-transfer into organic solvents the fluorescence property of the extracted CQDs is excitation-independent, the fluorescence intensity increases by 3–12 times as compared to the CQDs before purification; the maximum excitation wavelength shifts to around 370 nm, while the maximum emission wavelength shifts to 422–434 nm. The quantum yield of the CQDs decreases to 45.1% after a purification by dialysis from the original 82.7% in the basic mixture solution, while it decreases to 57.5% after a purification of phase-transfer.

The purified CQDs by phase-transfer into organic solvent is composed of a pure crystalline carbon core and surface-grafted groups of hydroxyl, ether, carbonyl, carboxylic and tiny sulfonate, while the purified CQDs by dialysis in water will adsorb additional L-cystine, sodium and by-product nitrate ions. Such a difference of the surface state of the CQDs, as well as the different solvent environment, should contribute to the different fluorescence property. In fact, the pure CQDs extracted in organic solvents shall have many advantages and will broaden their practical applications as labeling and emitting materials in the future.

Jie Zhu: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Chunxing Wu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Yongmei Cui: Formal analysis, Software. Dongxiang Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Yaojun Zhang: Data curation, Software, Investigation. Jie Xu: Methodology, Resources. Chunfang Li: Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Shahid Iqbal: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Meiwen Cao: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NNSFC 21975139), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017MB042), China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.126673.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
